schools.summary: Cyril: the Galileans, followers of Judas of Galilee (Acts 5:37) who refused Caesar, slain by Pilate among their sacrifices; the rulers report it to test Christ and excite hatred; Bede: the warning fulfilled in Rome's destruction of the nation.
The report arises from Christ's preaching on the punishment of sinners; he turns the tragedy into a call to repentance for all his hearers.

Christ sweeps away the rash inference that sudden death proves peculiar guilt (John 9:2; Job 4:7) — this is not a world of retribution, good and evil mingled; he turns the matter inward to a delicate yet severe call to repent.

The Galileans not greater sinners; 'except ye repent' a present subjunctive (continual turning); 'ye shall all likewise perish' a precise prophecy fulfilled in A.D. 70, when the nation perished by the Roman sword at a festival, buried under the temple's ruins (Wesley; 2 Pet 3:9).

Christ adds a parallel — the tower in Siloam (the pool, John 9:7; Neh 3:15; perhaps part of Pilate's aqueduct works from the Corban, Ellicott); the term 'sinners' here is 'debtors' (opheiletai, debtors to the law) vs the Galileans' hamartōloi; an accident no proof of greater guilt.

The denial and call repeated; not a world of retribution, but God is just and the future world will equalize all (Barnes); 'except ye repent' here aorist (immediate) vs present (continual) in v.3; fulfilled when Rome slew the priests at their sacrifices and buried thousands under the temple (Clarke; Matt 24:2).

The parable answers Jewish presumption (Titus); the fig tree = the synagogue, all leaves and no fruit (Ambrose), or mankind (Augustine, recalling Adam's fig leaves, Gen 3:7), or each soul (Theophylact); the owner (Christ) came seeking the fruits of faith and found none (Bede), after coming to Abraham, Moses, and in the flesh (Ambrose).
Israel planted in God's vineyard (Isa 5:1), tended through the ages, visited at last and found fruitless for all its leaves.

The dresser = Christ or the ministers; 'three years' a long patient waiting (or Christ's ministry, Clarke); 'cumbereth the ground' = renders it sterile, not merely useless but harmful (like hypocritical professors); the law against felling fruit trees (Deut 20:19; Matt 7:19); Barnes maps the allegory (God, the Jews, the delay of vengeance).

The dresser's intercession (Christ's or the ministers', like Abraham for Sodom, Moses for Israel, Ps 106:23); 'let it alone this year' the delay of vengeance (2 Pet 3:9; Rom 2:4); digging and dunging the means of grace.
Ambrose/Theophylact: the digging = the apostles' spades and the teaching of humility (the dug ground is lowly), the dung = the grace of humility that makes the fig fruitful.

The aposiopesis ('if it bear fruit' — 'well' supplied) leaves the longed-for outcome unspoken (Matt 3:10); fruit a blessing to all; 'if not, cut it down' the final judgment (John 15:2; Heb 6:8) — a reprieve, not a reversal.
Bede: the cutting down fulfilled under the Romans, the nation thrust from the land of promise.
Ambrose/Gregory/Augustine bind the bowed woman to the fig-tree by number (3×6=18), figuring man bowed before, under, and at the dawn of grace; Christ sometimes calls but does not make upright.
Ellicott: the narrative is peculiar to Luke, likely a gathered tradition; Robertson notes the periphrastic imperfect 'was teaching.'
Calvin: an extraordinary disease 'held by a spirit'; Christ's divine power triumphs over a Satan permitted only so far as God grants.
Henry: bodily infirmities, unless very grievous, should not keep us from sabbath worship — there the woman met her healer.
Luke's medical words: συνκύπτουσα = spinal curvature; Ellicott reads the 'spirit of infirmity' as an evil spirit acting where soul and body interact.
Calvin: extraordinary diseases are often inflicted through Satan (by God's permission), magnifying Christ's triumphant power.
Clarke traces origin (sin), agent (Satan), and duration; Wesley wonders if a modern physician would call it a nervous case.

debates.question: Is the infirmity demonic or merely a natural disease?

sides.position: truly Satan's binding, by God's permission
would now be called a natural/nervous disorder

schools.summary: Barnes: an unsought cure proving Messiahship; Robertson reads the perfect ἀπολέλυσαι (loosed to stay free); Ellicott hears a test of faith, the healing already complete.
Calvin: a double proof of power and grace — Christ declares deliverance at his own disposal and adds the sign of his touch.
Robertson: the touch of the Great Physician, effective aorist 'made straight,' inchoative imperfect 'began to glorify'; Ellicott: the touch a help to faith.
Calvin: the public praise shows an undoubted heavenly blessing, which only sharpens the cavillers' malignity.
Robertson: the ruler's unprompted, self-important indignation and ludicrous logic; Ellicott: the rabbinic rule allowed healing only in emergencies, not chronic cases.
Calvin: he dares not censure Christ openly but vents venom on the crowd, limiting God's grace on the very day for seeking it.
Henry: chesed means both godly and merciful — works of mercy are works of piety, most proper on the sabbath.
A crushing reply from the Jews' own Sabbath practice; Lightfoot grounds it in the rabbinic rules permitting watering cattle on the sabbath.
Calvin: a twofold comparison — the beast with the woman, the halter with Satan's chains; humanity to cattle shames refusal of mercy to God's child.
Wesley: the real motive was envy, not zeal for God's glory.
Robertson: a triple argument (human, woman, daughter of Abraham) and 'ought not' = necessity; Ellicott: disease traced to the Enemy, a comfort to Luke the physician.
Gill: she is a daughter of Abraham spiritually too, walking in his faith, now a believer in Christ.
Wesley presses the a fortiori — a human, a daughter of Abraham, much more than an ox, ought to be loosed.
Robertson: the contrast of imperfects — adversaries 'put to shame,' people 'rejoiced'; Barnes: the rulers oppose, the common people hear gladly.
Henry: a shame that works indignation not repentance, yet a sign all Christ's foes will be ashamed; his honour is the Christian's comfort.
Cyril of Jerusalem digresses on the Spirit breathed on the apostles.

Ambrose/Gloss/Bede/Augustine: the mustard seed is faith, the martyrs, and Christ himself sown and risen; the leaven is Christ, or love, hid in three measures (mankind, or Father-Son-Spirit, or body-soul-spirit).
Barnes/Clarke/Wesley defer to Matt 13:31–33; Robertson notes the repetition and the unclear 'therefore.'
Cyril/Theophylact: the kingdom/Gospel at first in few, then spread abroad; the seed sown in the garden of the soul.
Robertson: the proverbial small seed, Luke's distinctive 'garden' and Hebraism 'became a tree'; Barnes defers to Matt 13:31.
Cyril of Jerusalem: as the small seed is 'fiery in operation' and shelters the fowls, so faith swiftly works the greatest effects in the soul.
Robertson and Barnes refer the leaven parable to Matt 13:33; the doubled question pairs outward growth (seed) with inward permeation (leaven).
Gill: the renewed saying introduces the parable of the leaven hid in three measures of meal.
Gill reads the leaven both ways — the Gospel's small, spreading grace, or false doctrine secretly corrupting the churches.
Barnes/Clarke defer to Matt 13:33; the leaven hidden in three measures works silently till the whole is leavened.
Wesley: 'covered up' — so that for a time nothing of it appeared.

debates.question: Does the leaven picture good (the Gospel) or corruption (false doctrine)?

schools.summary: Ellicott/Robertson: the continued Peraean journey to Jerusalem; Barnes notes Christ's diligence even while travelling.
Gregory: Christ answers not the idle 'few or many?' but the profitable 'how to enter'; Augustine harmonizes 'few' (vs the lost) with 'many' (with the angels).
Cyril/Chrysostom: the narrow gate is the saints' toil, yet easy to those who undertake it heartily.
Ellicott: 'are being saved' (present); the question is speculative, reflecting the age's anxieties; Christ puts it aside and presses personal striving.
Clarke: the grand question is not 'are few saved?' but 'Can I be saved?' — agonize, exert every power.
Calvin: Christ withdraws his people from a curiosity that would be saved only 'in a crowd' and warns of the appalling difficulty of the way.
'Strive' = agonize, from the Grecian games; the strait gate here is the narrow door of the house; 'shall not be able' points to seeking too late.
Clarke/Wesley: many seek (wish) but do not strive (agonize); barely seeking will not avail.
Calvin: a warning against the vain hope that a crowd of companions or a presumed certainty will gain entrance.
Barnes: a proper time to enter, then too late at death; Robertson: 'slammed the door fast,' possibly a second parable about being too late.
Calvin: the householder shuts out night-prowlers; we must seize the open door while invited, for it will at length be shut.
Wesley: even agonizing will not avail after the door is shut — agonize now by faith, prayer, holiness.
Barnes: false confidence resting on profession and outward communion, which will not avail without a changed heart; Ellicott: not office-holders but those who merely ate with Christ.
Calvin: familiar intercourse profits nothing if, when called, they do not answer; Christ now speaks plainly as the judge.
Robertson: the blunt sentence sweeps away false pleas — acquaintance in the flesh opens no door; echoes Ps 6:8 as in Matt 7:23.
Gill hears the Master's abhorrence — 'be gone… be far from my door.'
Robertson: 'there' = outside the shut door, 'being cast out' continuous; Barnes/Clarke refer to Matt 8:11–12.
Calvin: descent from the fathers profits nothing; a race departed from their faith has no inheritance (Eph 5:5).
Cyril of Jerusalem: hope even for the repentant publican and harlot — ignorance is pardoned, persistent wickedness condemned.
Robertson: the heavenly banquet, many coming from the four quarters, not saved in different ways; Clarke: the Gentiles receive what the Jews reject.
Calvin: the Gentiles substituted for the rejected Jews, to provoke them to a holy jealousy (Rom 11).
Ellicott: the first utterance of the law that God's judgment reverses man's, applied primarily to the nations; Gill: the abject Gentiles made first.
Calvin: here it throws down the Jews' vain confidence in their election-by-priority; the Gentiles take the first rank.
Wesley applies it individually — late-called Gentiles highly rewarded, first-called Jews unrewarded.
Ellicott canvasses three views and (with Robertson, Barnes) judges the warning a ruse — Pharisees plotting, perhaps sent by Herod with Herodias's hand.
Calvin: the Pharisees sought to frighten Christ into hiding because the people were attached to him; behind them, Satan.
Cyril/Bede: the Pharisees feared loss of office and gains; the 'fox' types crafty heretics who would destroy Christ in believers' hearts.

debates.question: Was the warning friendly, a Pharisaic ruse, or sent by Herod?

sides.position: a ruse to frighten Jesus away (most probable)

schools.summary: 'Fox' = cunning and cowardice; Ellicott notes the feminine form (Herod unmanned by Herodias); 'three days' proverbial; 'perfected' = course ended in suffering.
Calvin: the 'fox' is Herod's insidious craft against the Gospel; Christ will discharge his office and then be offered.
Wesley: Christ's extraordinary office justified such severity toward wicked princes, to which we have no call.

debates.question: Who is 'that fox' — Herod or the Pharisee messenger?

sides.position: Herod, for his craft and cowardice
possibly the messenger who brought the advice

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'I must journey' — fixed purpose, 'it cannot be' half-ironical; Jerusalem's monopoly on slaying prophets; Lightfoot adds the Sanhedrin's jurisdiction.
Calvin: a sharp attack on the Pharisees — Jerusalem a den of robbers where the prophets were butchered.

debates.question: Are 'to day and to morrow' literal days or proverbial?

schools.summary: Bede: Christ weeps over the dwellers with a father's love; the doubled name betokens compassion (Chrysostom); the gathering is the long history of grace refused (Cyril, Augustine).
Robertson: 'how often' shows repeated visits; the lament may be spoken both here and in Jerusalem; νοσσιάν vs νοσσία.
Wesley counts three solemn visits since the baptism for this very purpose.

debates.question: Did Christ utter this lament once or twice (here and in Matt 23)?

sides.position: twice — spoken at different times and places
once, inserted by Luke on the mention of Jerusalem

schools.summary: Clarke: 'your house' = the abandoned temple; Barnes ties desolation to the prophets and 'Blessed is he' to the triumphal entry; Lightfoot owns the difficulty of 'ye shall not see me.'
Wesley: the house irrecoverably desolate; they shall long for the Messiah in their calamities, not necessarily see Jesus.
Theophylact refers 'see me' to after the cross; Augustine/Cyril/Bede to the second coming, on repentance.

debates.question: When is the 'until ye shall say, Blessed is he'?

sides.position: the second coming, willing or unwilling confession
